data.title: The MSC Income Fund Is Not On Par With Main Street Capital
data.description:
This year, business development companies (BIZD) have been a centerpiece of my coverage. The turmoil in direct lending has caused widespread fears across the private credit sphere and it's no secret that some non-traded business development companies have run into trouble as investors head for the hills. I recently published an article outlining the contagion effects in private credit that are beginning to weigh on publicly traded business development companies.

Just as it went with REITs when rates increased, private capital quickly faces redemption gates which put market pressure on more liquid counterparts. This factor has pushed the sector median valuation for business development companies much lower over the past year.

In today's discussion, we will compare the MSC Income Fund (MSIF) against its own BDC manager, Main Street Capital (MAIN), and explain why MSIF may be the inferior pick. MSIF went public just a short while ago and appears to have hit the public markets at a tumultuous time. Today, I will initiate coverage of MSIF, provide my take on the fund, and explain why MAIN might be a superior choice for those looking for a BDC investment.

A Short History Lesson

Before we dive into MSIF, we need to understand the relationship between MAIN and MSIF. Over the years, I have covered MAIN as a centerpiece of the business development company sector. Aside from operating a top notch business, MAIN has outperformed the sector and market as a whole over a long time period.

MAIN is an internally managed business development company that went public in 2007. Over the years, the company has differentiated itself through an equity heavy portfolio strategy that has paid dividends over the years, quite literally. The fund has been one of the more unique business development companies in that net asset value has grown significantly over time allowing the company to grow its dividend and provide special dividends to shareholders. Within the business development company space, MAIN has historically focused on smaller, but mature businesses. In the words of the company, they focus on businesses that are older than they are.

This successful track record has helped the company amass nearly $9 billion under management. Around 80% of this capital sits under the umbrella of the fund at MAIN. The remaining portion was mostly held in a non-traded business development company to which MAIN was the fund advisor. This smaller piece allowed MAIN to act as an advisor providing asset management services that include sourcing, due diligence, and investment management. Meanwhile, the former partner to the fund handled fundraising, balance sheet management, and accounting and administrative services.

At the time, the fund was called the Main Street Income Fund. In October 2020, MAIN became the sole advisor to the fund and the name changed to the MSC Income Fund. At this point, MAIN had itself its own non-traded business development company of which it was the external asset manager. MAIN is a business development company that is internally managed. This means that the company does not charge a management and incentive fee to assets under management but rather operates more like a traditional business. Within that internally managed business sat this externally managed fund of which MAIN was the fund advisor and collected management fees, a boost to their earnings.

MAIN's investor presentation quotes that this reduces their effective expenses on total assets to 1.3%. Compare this point to MAIN's estimate of other BDC's which have an effective expense ratio of around 2.5%.

Last year, MAIN took this non-traded BDC public under the ticker symbol MSIF.

So, to summarize, MSIF was formerly a non-traded business development company under the umbrella of MAIN. Following the IPO at the beginning of last year, it became a separate, externally managed, publicly traded business development company that continues to be overseen by the team at MAIN. This opens up immediate liquidity for the fund's investors. Rather than being held under the internal expense structure of MAIN, MSIF pays asset management fees to MAIN.

MAIN discusses the benefit to their business on the Q4 Earnings Call.

Funds we advised through our external investment manager continued to experience favorable performance in the fourth quarter, resulting in significant incentive fee income for our asset management business, and together with our recurring base management fees, a significant contribution to our net investment income.

MSIF's current base management fee is 150 basis points according to the most recent investor presentation. As MSIF continues to grow, this will become a growing piece of MAIN's business.

This should hopefully provide a quick overview of this short but exciting history of this externally managed business development company. With that, let's go ahead and dive into the fund and see how it stacks up against other BDC's.

Fund Overview

Since MSIF is an independent fund from MAIN, we should assess it on its own. The fund manages a total investment portfolio of about $1.3 billion. Like the original MAIN, the fund has a split between debt investments and equity investments. However, unlike its older sibling, this fund has a much smaller portion of the portfolio allocated towards equity at around 20%.

I would venture to guess as the fund begins to age and move towards the maturity of its publicly traded stage, we will see a much, much smaller equity portion within the portfolio. This assertion is supported by the fund's website which explains that MSIF differs from MAIN through what the manager calls a "private loan only investment strategy"

MSIF differs from Main Street Capital through its private loan only investment strategy and focus on providing current returns through current dividends.

This is an important distinction to draw as two key drivers of man's ability to grow net asset value over time have been appreciating equity investments and the ability to leverage a premium valuation to issue shares well above net asset value. Without a substantial equity sleeve to support operations, MSIF looks more like other business development companies that are publicly traded.

As is typical with the MAIN secret recipe, MSIF invests and established, profitable companies. Below is a screenshot from the most recent investor presentation that talks about the private loan, lower middle market, and aggregate portfolio investments.

As one would expect with any business development company, investments are spread across a wide variety of clients. The total portfolio has nearly 150 companies with an average investment size of approximately $9 million. Private loans make up around 61% of assets and lower middle market comprises around 36%. The remaining small portion is allocated to the middle market and other portfolio investments.

Right now, the fund is well diversified across industries with technology comprising a relatively small component of the overall portfolio. Having a larger portion of the portfolio exposed to fixed rate debt means the fund is more sensitive to movements in benchmark interest rates as well. MSIF provides a SOFR sensitivity that shows net investment income per share moves alongside SOFR.

Most of these loans will come from MAIN's deals as MSIF continues to shift towards their debt only strategy. Specifically, the fund's private loan strategy which is the largest piece of the portfolio is directly tied to MAIN.

Private Loan portfolio investments are primarily debt investments in privately held companies which have primarily been originated directly by Main Street or, to a lesser extent, through Main Street's strategic relationships with other credit funds on a collaborative basis (often referred to in the debt markets as "club deals" because of the small lender group size)

Let's explore this relationship more deeply.

Putting The Pieces Together

This relationship becomes collaborative with MAIN, who can retain the equity position while having the opportunity to sell loans directly into MSIF instead of finding an external buyer.

Think of it this way, MAIN is a private equity engine which has been able to assemble a large portfolio of companies that they control. A cornerstone of MAIN's business has been collecting dividends from these companies which helps support the normal and special distribution paid by MAIN shares. Over time, debt investments have remained a large portion of man's investment strategy. This separate externally managed pool was formerly comprised of external capital. It provided an opportunity for MAIN to allocate their investments and other investments they come across into an external capital pool where it might not necessarily fit on their own balance sheet.

Overtime, this flexible strategy has been adapted by other companies including Realty income which has recently introduced a non-traded REIT to their own platform under a similar concept.

The issue that was facing MAIN with this externally managed, non-traded business development company may have been liquidity. By having external investors, these investors may want to pull their capital, something which we have seen happening on an increasing basis. This year, redemption gates have been coming up quickly for non-traded business development companies as investors quickly try to pull out their capital. By converting to a permanent public structure MSIF is able to guarantee a permanent capital structure which is advantageous for the long term hold period expected for lower middle market investments. This is a huge advantage for a fund advisor at a time like this when liquidity becomes an issue.

However, this means the new company is exposed to the public markets and their corresponding turmoil. Unfortunately for investors who piled in quickly, this has quickly translated to a discounted valuation as the fund's price continues to move below net asset value.

MSIF shares continue to trade at a discount to net asset value alongside other business development companies as the sector faces trouble.

Many see this as a huge opportunity as MAIN itself continues to trade far above its own net asset value.

Let me explain why this is so important for MAIN. Over the past few years, MAIN has been rapidly issuing shares at a premium to net asset value.

This capital market activity in and of itself raises net asset value as I have explained in my coverage of MAIN.

Historically, MAIN has earned this valuation because of its ability to grow net asset value with the equity sleeve. Then it compounded when the equity sleeve worked alongside share issuances as a double whammy.

Now, it appears that MAIN is only able to grow NAV through this capital market activity as the going gets tough. As nonaccruals continue to grow and valuations come under pressure, net asset value growth is carrying the entire load for NAV growth. See below from MAIN's recent preliminary NAV estimates.

The estimated NAV per share increase is primarily due to the accretive impact of equity issuances, partially offset by the net fair value decrease of the existing investment portfolio, the total dividends per share paid in the first quarter in excess of NII per share and the net tax provision.

Well, trading at a material discount to net asset value means that MSIF simply cannot pull this same lever to deliver returns to shareholders. What quickly begins to look even worse is the fund's pivot deeper and deeper into debt investments while nonaccrual begins to rise.

Investments on nonaccrual have almost reached 4% of cost, nearly matching that of MAIN itself. Right now, this leaves MSIF in a disadvantageous position relative to MAIN in terms of their abilities to grow net asset value.

Recently, my assessment of MAIN has been somewhat negative, earning a Sell rating due to valuation concerns.

More recently, shifts in pricing have roughly realigned share price with historical price to book value. With the calculus around these two shifting, the picture begins to look somewhat different. For example, today, MAIN trades at around 1.5x forward book value, below the 1.67x five year average for MAIN shares.

Investor Takeaways

When people are looking at MSIF and MAIN, I am concerned that people believe they are similar. While there are some folks who call them MAIN and mini-MAIN, I believe a better comparison is Gladstone Capital (GLAD) relative to Gladstone Investment (GAIN) which have similarly differentiated strategies. Looking at these two specifically, we can see just how far performance can diverge over time.

When people are comparing MSIF to MAIN, it's important to incorporate the difference in management style, portfolio strategy, and balance sheet. These critical differences are likely to become the determining factor in long term performance when combined with the fund's ability to leverage capital markets activity and manage price relative to net asset value. All of these factors amount to considerable risks for a fund which is yet to prove itself as a publicly traded vehicle. With expenses that are considerably higher than MAIN and a less attractive strategy overall, MSIF earns a Sell rating, while MAIN earns a Hold based on current pricing.

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners: The Valuation Remains Challenging
data.description:
With the Iran war still threatening to spark a bout of stagflation, trading conditions could get more difficult for Europe's consumer-facing businesses. That being the case, honing in on those firms with the strongest brands may not be the worst idea in the world.

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners PLC (CCEP) is one possible candidate. Few brands are as iconic as its namesake, and while CCEP doesn't actually own it, its status as the largest Coke bottler in the world puts it on the front line in terms of consumer demand.

I last covered CCEP in 2024. Downgrading it to "Hold", my major issue was with its valuation. As expected, CCEP's volumes had been holding up well following meaningful price hikes, but given the low-growth nature of its markets and beverage portfolio, I didn't want to pay more than 15x earnings for the shares.

Returning around 35%, they have done well since then. Some of that is due to currency (the dollar has weakened), but re-rating has also played a role, with CCEP now trading for around 20x trailing earnings, up from 18.5x last time. Frankly, this remains a sticking point for me. With better value nearby in the Coca-Cola system, I continue to rate CCEP "Hold."

Brand Strength

To quickly recap, CCEP is one of the largest bottlers in the Coca-Cola system. Its business model is pretty simple. The company buys ingredients and finished goods under license agreements from the better-known Coca-Cola Company (KO), turns them into end products, and then ships them to commercial customers in various channels (supermarkets, wholesalers, hotels, and so on).

Last year, CCEP booked €21 billion in revenue. Around 75% came from Europe, where CCEP operates in mature northern and western markets. The rest was generated in Asia and Oceania, where CCEP has exposure to faster-growing emerging markets like Indonesia and the Philippines.

Unfortunately, the economic outlook in Europe is highly uncertain. Just like in 2022, when the Russian invasion of Ukraine triggered a surge in inflation, the conflict in Iran is threatening the same. CCEP's non-European markets aren't immune either. In fact, they may fare even worse. The Philippines, for example, introduced a four-day week last month to conserve energy.

It helps that the Coca-Cola brand is so strong. Put simply, private label penetration is quite low in the soda category, making Coke relatively more immune to down-trading compared to other food & beverage products.

Evidence to support this can be found in CCEP and KO's recent historical results. Between 2023 and 2025, for example, CCEP managed to deliver flat comparable unit case growth despite growing its revenue per case by an average of 4%. KO's results are even starker. Over the same period, it delivered 1%-2% average unit case growth while delivering 8% average growth in price/mix.

Two Structural Reasons For Caution

Now, you will note above that the drivers of CCEP's top line have been weaker than KO's. There are two structural reasons contributing to this. Firstly, CCEP's operating footprint largely covers very mature beverage markets. As you saw in the last section, Great Britain, France, Germany, Spain, and Portugal account for well over half its revenue. These markets have unfavorable demographics and mature rates of soda consumption.

By contrast, Indonesia and the Philippines enjoy better prospects. Demographics are healthier, as is the outlook for consumption, given low per capita rates compared to Western standards (shown below). Unfortunately, these two markets only account for around 12% of CCEP's revenue, making their impact relatively limited.

The second reason is related to CCEP's beverage mix. Classic Coca-Cola is around 35-40% of its volume, rising to 60% including low/zero-sugar formulations like Coke Zero and Diet Coke. Flavors and Mixers, which includes non-Coke soda brands like Sprite and Fanta, is another 20% of its volume. That makes for a total skew to the sparkling sub-category of approximately 80%. Now, while this helps CCEP fend off competition from private label products, it does come with a flip side, which is that the sparkling category has one of the weakest growth outlooks in the coming years.

Combined, these points do not make for an exciting revenue outlook for CCEP. Indeed, sales only grew by around 3% last year after adjusting for currency and the impact of M&A. Analysts expect a similar rate of growth through 2028, including in its Q1 2026 update at the end of the month.

It's true that profit growth has been higher than this. CCEP posted €2.81 billion in operating income last year, up 7% after adjusting for currency and M&A. Analysts expect a similar rate of growth through 2028. Operating leverage is part of this, while CCEP has been able to extract cost efficiencies throughout its business (more automation in its warehouses, for example). Eventually, though, earnings growth will revert closer to revenue growth.

War Impacts Won't Show Up Just Yet

Having mentioned the upcoming Q1 report, it's worth spending a brief moment to discuss the implications of events in the Middle East. Before that, I should point out that this will only be a trading update—meaning we won't get a comprehensive set of financial data (only figures for revenue).

With that, analysts expect 9% currency-neutral revenue growth, driven by 7.5% volume growth. Now, these figures seem high in light of what I set out in the last section, but this growth is really just a phasing effect related to how CCEP's financial calendar works. It was mentioned on the previous earnings call, but to cut a long story short, there will be six extra selling days in Q1 2026.

I am not expecting any major headwinds from Iran to show up in this update. Not only did the conflict begin towards the end of the quarter, but recent commentary from British supermarket chain Tesco (TSCDY) suggests that consumers have yet to alter their behavior. Note that Britain is CCEP's biggest market, and as Tesco is the largest supermarket chain there, its insights are relevant to CCEP.

Look, let me keep it in simple terms. So far, we haven't seen any real discernible change in consumer spending behaviors, right? You see that in our Kantar data. You see it in both the volume and the value share. I feel good about how we started the year, but it's early days, and I would say to you that we aim to grow profits every single year.

Imran Nawaz, Tesco CFO & Director.

On the flip side, GDP forecasts from organizations like the IMF have been lowered, and that could obviously weigh on consumer demand later in the year. Note also that CCEP has already hedged around 80% of its 2026 commodity costs, and these account for around 25% of its direct costs. This will limit the impact of any price shocks on its cost base, at least for now.

Given the above, it would not surprise me if CCEP widened its guidance to account for the extra uncertainty, but I am not expecting major revisions at this point.

A Puzzling Premium

The above is key to assessing CCEP's valuation. At $96.78 (around €82 in its operating currency), the shares trade for roughly 20x last year's adjusted EPS (€4.11). Not only does this look lofty in its own right, but this multiple is also puzzling compared to one of its closest bottling peers, Coca-Cola HBC (CCHGY)(CCHBF), which I covered last week. This is what I was referring to when I said that there was better value nearby in the Coca-Cola system.

Like CCEP, Coca-Cola HBC also has a large European footprint, albeit it operates in southern, central, and eastern markets. However, around 27% of Coca-Cola HBC's volumes come from Nigeria and Egypt, where both demographics and consumption rates are tailwinds rather than headwinds. Coca-Cola HBC has also agreed to a deal to buy Coca-Cola Beverages Africa ('CCBA'), which will add another 14 African markets to its list of territories. Post-deal, over 40% of Coca-Cola HBC's volume will come from Africa.

In addition to that, I would also add that Coca-Cola HBC's portfolio is slightly less reliant on the sparkling sub-category, which accounts for around 67% of its volume. By contrast, it has slightly more exposure to faster-growing products like energy drinks (9% of the portfolio versus 5% for CCEP). Consistent with all of this, analysts expect Coca-Cola HBC to grow its revenue and operating profit at a faster clip than CCEP in the coming years.

You might expect this growth to attract a higher valuation from the market. In fact, Coca-Cola HBC's ADSs trade for $59.25 (around €50 in its operating currency), equal to around 18.5x last year's adjusted EPS (€2.72). While there are other factors that influence valuation, these don't appear to explain things either. Leverage, for example, is roughly comparable, with CCEP sitting on €9.8 billion in net debt at the end of 2025, equal to roughly 2.7x that year's EBITDA. Coca-Cola HBC's leverage was only 0.7x at the end of last year, though it is set to rise to 2.5x EBITDA post-CCBA.

Given inferior growth, comparable leverage (pro forma for the CCBA deal), and a higher P/E ratio, I don't see a compelling reason for prospective investors to favor CCEP over Coca-Cola HBC at this point. Accordingly, I leave my "Hold" rating for CCEP unchanged.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Why I See Downside Ahead
data.description:
So, since my last article, "Johnson & Johnson: Strong Run May Be Ending," which, by the way, caused an extremely negative reaction from you, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) aka J&J stock has fallen 4.2%, underperforming the S&P 500 [1.8% return] (SPY).

And one of the reasons behind this is the mixed Q1 numbers. Though it is not obvious at first glance.

Yes, its revenue grew 9.9% year-over-year to $24.06 billion, beating the consensus estimate by $447 million. And yes, its non-GAAP EPS of $2.7 was also higher than what Wall Street analysts expected, but just by 2 cents.

And so, I have a question. Is J&J doing well with sales of its immunology, oncology, and neurology franchises?

Or is there still a divergence between sales of its key blockbusters and the price of its shares, which prevents an upgrade rating of Johnson & Johnson under my “ALLKA 4D Rating” model?

J&J's immunology franchise

First of all, let me remind you that Tremfya and Stelara are two key drugs in its portfolio.

There's also Remicade [infliximab].

But biosimilars to it have already been launched.

And Alvotech/Teva Pharmaceuticals' AVT05 (ALVO, TEVA) and Bio-Thera's BAT2506 are awaiting FDA approval. By the way, AVT05/BAT2506 are biosimilars to J&J's golimumab.

So, as I said earlier, Tremfya [guselkumab] is the successor to the fast-fading blockbuster Stelara.

Since 2025, 8 "cheap copies" of Stelara have already been launched, including Amgen's Wezlana (AMGN), Samsung Bioepis/Sandoz's Pyzchiva (SDZNY), and Biocon Biologics' Yesintek.

That is why sales of this IL-12/23 inhibitor fell by 59.6% YoY and 46.7% quarter-over-quarter to $656 million, and also missed even my "base case" scenario by $55 million.

On the other hand, Tremfya, a first anti-IL-23 mAb, showed modest performance, even though the FDA on September 29 last year approved its subcutaneous version for the treatment of children over 6 years of age with two forms of immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, plaque psoriasis and active psoriatic arthritis.

I wrote "modest performance" for a reason.

So, Tremfya generated $1.61 billion in revenue for J&J, a 1.2% quarter-on-quarter growth.

The slowdown in its sales, in my view, reflects the preference of gastroenterologists for AbbVie's Rinvoq/Skyrizi (ABBV) and Takeda's (TAK) Entyvio.

And also, the FDA approved Eli Lilly's (LLY) Omvoh on January 15 of last year for the treatment of Crohn's disease.

The oncology franchise has once again demonstrated strong performance

I can say for sure that J&J is the undisputed leader in the multiple myeloma and lung cancer therapeutics market.

And behind this lies the "Fantastic Four": Darzalex/Darzalex Faspro [daratumumab], Lazcluze/Rybrevant, Carvykti, and Tecvayli.

But I'll only discuss daratumumab and Carvykti, as they are J&J's 2 best-selling cancer drugs, if I don’t take Imbruvica [ibrutinib] into account.

Overall, as you may know, Imbruvica is under pressure from more effective inhibitors of BTK, BeOne Medicines' Brukinsa (ONC) and AstraZeneca's Calquence (AZN). That’s why ibrutinib revenues were down 6.9% YoY.

But this is not all.

In my opinion, it's also important to pay attention to the factors that could cause stock trading at a non-GAAP P/E ratio [FWD] higher than their sector median and 5-year average, as in J&J's case [15.6% and 24%, respectively], to fall in price.

So I'll continue with Darzalex.

At first glance, everything looks great. The daratumumab franchise generated $3.96 billion in sales for Johnson & Johnson in Q1, up 22.5% year-over-year.

But this morning, I created the following scatter plot, which helped me put all the pieces of the puzzle together.

What do I see here, below?

Before I answer, I'll explain how this chart works, just in case. The text above the circles indicates the quarter I'm comparing it to.

For example, the purple dot indicates on the X-axis how sales of Darzalex/Darzalex Faspro changed in Q1 2026 compared to Q4 2025 [grew by $61 million]. And if you do the same on the Y-axis, it will show the change in sales YoY, that is, Q1 2026 relative to Q1 2025 [up $727 million]. The colors of the circles indicate the year [red is 2025 and purple is 2026].

And yes, VP Darren Snellgrove sounded quite enthusiastic about Darzalex on the call.

In oncology, starting with multiple myeloma, DARZALEX growth was 17.8%, primarily driven by strong share gains of 5.9 points across all lines of therapy, with nearly 12 points in the frontline setting as well as market growth.

Therefore, I can only assume that the slowdown in the sales of the daratumumab franchise is either a one-off incident or related to oncologists starting to choose Sanofi's Sarclisa (SNY), also the anti-CD38 therapy as Darzalex, and J&J's other anti-multiple myeloma [MM] agents, Tecvayli and Carvykti.

That's why I'll continue talking about Carvykti [ciltacabtagene autoleucel].

This CAR T-cell therapy is approved as 2L+ relapsed and lenalidomide-refractory MM.

I have already mentioned how Carvykti works in previous articles, but its important advantage compared to Darzalex or Talvey or Pfizer's Elrexfio (PFE) is that it is a one-time infusion.

And, in my view, also because of the expansion of manufacturing capacity, including at the Tech Lane facility in Ghent, Belgium, and the Raritan facility, NJ, Carvykti sales rose by 61.8% YoY and by 7.6% quarter-on-quarter to $597 million in Q1.

But again, there's a small detail I should mention, namely, J&J only earns 50% from Carvykti sales, under the collaboration agreement with Legend Biotech (LEGN).

Thus, I believe, although I would like to point out that this is only my subjective opinion, that Legend is a suitable acquisition target, especially in anticipation of the results of the two Phase 3s, CARTITUDE-5 and CARTITUDE-6.

I base my opinion on the fact that LEGN's market capitalization is only $3.8 billion, even though it has a rich portfolio of product candidates in addition to Carvykti. For example, LCAR-AIO [R/R autoimmune diseases] or LB2102 [large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma and small cell lung cancer].

And moreover, Gilead (GILD) acquired Legend's competitor, Arcellx (ACLX), for $7.8 billion, even though its anti-BCMA CAR T-cell agent, anito-cel, is still under review by the FDA.

Takeaway

Johnson & Johnson is the "gold standard" among Big Pharma players for income-oriented investors.

But I started to see several warning signs.

As I mentioned in the article, three of the four drugs in J&J's immunology franchise, Stelara, Remicade, and Simponi/Simponi Aria, are experiencing or will soon experience pressure from their biosimilars in the US.

For the first time in 9 years, it increased its quarterly dividends by only 4 cents.

I also didn't hear any significant progress on resolving over 70,000 talc cases on the earnings call.

As a result, I continue to cover J&J with a Sell rating.

However, I must point out that I may reconsider it if the stock reaches the +1σ band formed from the VWAP [$214.2]. Or if Johnson & Johnson finally buys out either Legend or Protagonist (PTGX), which will rejuvenate its pipeline.
